summary: McKenzie describes his experience testing the trendy 'madlibs-style' signup form that swept the internet after a viral case study. Rather than blindly adopting the design fad, he ran his own A/B test on Bingo Card Creator's registration form. The madlibs style resulted in a 22% decrease in conversion rates compared to the standard form, significant at the 95% confidence level. He emphasizes that other people's successful experiments should inspire you to run your own tests, not to copy their results wholesale. The original viral test changed seven variables simultaneously, making it impossible to attribute the improvement to the madlibs format specifically.
key_points: Trust your own A/B test data, not someone else's — you only make money when it improves your conversion rate, not theirs
The original viral madlibs test changed roughly seven things simultaneously, making it impossible to isolate which change actually drove the improvement
McKenzie's isolated test of just the madlibs style showed a 22% decrease in conversions, reaching statistical significance in only 34 hours
Having an A/B testing framework that makes tests trivial to run (a one-line proposition) removes the excuse for not testing before adopting trends
Design fads spread virally but their results don't transfer across contexts — what works for lead generation forms may hurt simple registration forms
money_quotes: There is a key point about A/B testing: trust your data, not somebody else's data.
You only make money when it improves your conversion rate, not their conversion rate.
You can feel free to use other folk's successful experiments for inspiration but for heaven's sake use them to inspire you to run tests, rather than inspire you to fire blindly.
When In Doubt, Test. (When Not In Doubt, Test Twice.)

I love the Internet because where else can you get 736 people to help you improve your website while you sleep, work at the day job on Saturday, have an evening out with friends, and then sleep some more?
Before adopting this or any other fad (or good suggestion, for that matter): do your own A/B tests.
key_insight: Other people's A/B test results should inspire you to run your own tests, not to blindly copy their implementations — context matters more than creativity.
